description.s: Investigate whether Automerge is the right storage foundation for Hobson's next phase: system-level undo + mobile sync.
Context
Two upcoming needs converge on the same problem:
System-level undo.
Deleting an item, removing an attachment, changing a type are all irreversible. The system needs a way to revert destructive operations.
Mobile sync.
Hobson needs to be accessible on mobile, which requires syncing data between devices with conflict resolution.
Automerge is a CRDT library that potentially solves both: its operation log gives history/undo, and its CRDT foundation enables sync with automatic conflict resolution.
Proposed Architecture
Automerge lives in the kernel storage layer. The rest of Hobson does not interact with Automerge directly.
Read path

A.load(bytes) → Automerge doc → convert to plain JS object → return to caller. Callers receive a mutable plain object, never an Automerge proxy. This is functionally necessary because callers mutate the returned object before passing it back to api.set().
Write path

Caller passes a plain JS object (as today) → storage layer diffs it against the current Automerge doc → applies minimal A.change() mutations at field level (not character level) → A.save() → bytes to IDB. This preserves the existing api.set(item) interface.
History and revert
History/revert belongs in the kernel storage layer, not as a userland item — item edits must not be able to break the history mechanism. The storage API would expose:
api.getHistory(itemId)
— list of changes with timestamps and messages
api.viewAt(itemId, heads)
— read-only plain object at a historical state (uses A.view(), which is cheap)
api.revertTo(itemId, heads)
— reverts item to a previous state by diffing and applying reverse patches
A userland history-view can call these APIs for the UI, but the mechanism itself is protected in the kernel.
Query

Automerge has no cross-document query mechanism. Hobson already maintains indexes in related-items-lib. The existing query/search pattern (load items, filter in JS) continues to work. A lightweight metadata index could avoid deserialising full items for bulk queries.
Open Questions
Read conversion performance.

api.get() returns a plain object clone (JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(doc))). This is sub-millisecond per item, but getAll() on 337+ items needs benchmarking. Alternative: return Automerge proxies directly for reads, clone only when caller needs mutability. Needs prototyping to see what breaks.
Write diffing complexity.

The storage layer must diff a plain JS object against an Automerge doc to produce fine-grained A.change() mutations. Object/primitive fields are straightforward. Arrays (tags, attachments, Active Text ASTs) need a diffing strategy — element-by-element comparison with fallback to full replacement. Needs prototyping.
Storage overhead.
Automerge stores full operation history. For Hobson's whole-field-value pattern, overhead is estimated at 2-3x raw JSON. Need to measure on real items.
WASM dependency.
Automerge's core is Rust compiled to WASM. Adds to initial load time and requires async initialisation. Need to measure impact on boot.
Undo for deleted items.

Reverting a field change is straightforward (diff + reverse patches). But reverting item deletion means the item's Automerge doc must still exist somewhere. May need soft-delete (mark as deleted, keep the doc) rather than actual removal.
Sync architecture.

What does mobile sync look like? automerge-repo handles document lifecycle and sync adapters. A sync server (or peer-to-peer via WebRTC) is needed. This is a later concern but the storage layer should not preclude it.
Alternatives.

PouchDB was considered but its revision system discards old bodies on compaction — designed for sync conflict resolution, not version history. Yjs is another CRDT option but has a different storage model. A custom IDB history store (copy-on-write) is simpler but doesn't help with sync.
Prototype Plan
Before committing to Automerge, build a minimal prototype to get hard numbers:
Take a representative large item (view with hundreds of lines of code, doc with a big Active Text AST).
Create an Automerge doc from it. Measure A.from() and A.save() sizes.
Measure JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(doc)) conversion time, singly and in bulk (337x).
Implement a naive field-level diff (old doc vs new plain object). Measure diffing + A.change() time.
Test Active Text AST mutations: insert block, change block type, edit text. Verify Automerge handles the nested structure correctly.
Measure A.getHistory(), A.view(), and A.diff() on a doc with ~50 changes.

If the prototype shows acceptable performance, proceed with integrating Automerge into the kernel storage layer. If not, evaluate whether proxy-based reads (skipping conversion) or a simpler custom approach is needed.
What We Know So Far
Automerge supports maps, lists, text (with rich-text marks), strings, booleans, null, integers, floats, counters, timestamps, bytes — a superset of JSON.
Documents read like plain JS objects (proxy-based). JSON.stringify() works directly.
Documents are immutable outside of A.change() — callers need a mutable clone, which is why the plain-object conversion on read is functionally necessary.
Active Text ASTs (nested arrays of mixed types) work natively. Fine-grained mutations on nested elements are supported. Replacing whole subtrees works but loses merge granularity.
A.view(doc, oldHeads) gives cheap read-only snapshots at any historical state (shares memory, no copying). A.diff() returns structured patches.
No built-in undo — implemented via patch inversion (capture reverse patches during A.change(), apply them to revert).
Storage: A.save() produces compact binary (Uint8Array) for IDB. A.load() restores. No need for automerge-repo for basic persistence.
Automerge 3.0 has 10x memory improvement over 2.0. Plain strings are collaborative by default; use A.ImmutableString for atomic strings (GUIDs, type IDs).
PouchDB was considered but rejected — its revision system discards old bodies on compaction, making it unsuitable for version history.
